--- title: RIP pandas, welcome Polars frontpic: img/polars_vs_pandas.png frontpicwidth: 40% frontpicmargintop: 30px frontpicmarginbottom: 20px author: Marie-Hélène Burle date: 2026-04-7 date-format: long execute: freeze: auto cache: true error: true echo: true format: revealjs: embed-resources: true theme: [default, ../revealjspdpl.scss] logo: /img/favicon_sfudrac.png highlight-style: vim-dark code-line-numbers: false template-partials: - ../title-slide.html pointer: color: "#b5111b" pointerSize: 32 link-external-newwindow: true footer:  Back to webinar page auto-stretch: false revealjs-plugins: - pointer --- ## Goal of this webinar {.center} There is a new and much better library for Python DataFrames There are no downsides to using it, beside the effort of changing habits. For new users who don't have habits yet, there are no downsides Yet, all Python intro courses still teach the old tool In this webinar, I will not teach Polars and its syntax. Instead I will demo why it is better [My goal is to help shift the culture towards a wider adoption of Polars instead of pandas]{.emph} ## A brief history of DataFrames {.center} ```{dot} //| echo: false //| fig-height: 450px strict graph { bgcolor="transparent" graph [fontname="Inconsolata, sans-serif"] node [fontname="Inconsolata, sans-serif", fontsize=15] y1 [label=1990, shape=plaintext, group=g1, group=g1] y2 [label=2000, shape=plaintext, group=g1, group=g1] y3 [label=2008, shape=plaintext, group=g1] y4 [label=2020, shape=plaintext, group=g1] l1 [label="S language introduces concept of DataFrames", href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S_(programming_language)", shape=plaintext, group=g2] l2 [label="R supercedes S and inherits DataFrames", href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/R_(programming_language)", shape=plaintext, group=g2] l3 [label="DataFrames ported to Python with pandas", href="https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas", shape=plaintext, group=g2, fontcolor="#e84c44"] l4 [label="Better DataFrames library for Python: Polars", href="https://github.com/pola-rs/polars", shape=plaintext, group=g2, fontcolor="#69c9e6"] {rank=same; y1 l1} y1 -- y2 -- y3 -- y4 l1 -- l2 -- l3 -- l4 [style=invis] } ``` ## Overview {.center} | | ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="55%"} || ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="50%"} | |--|--|--|--| | Available for | Python || Rust, Python, R, NodeJS | | Written in | Cython || Rust | | Multithreading | Some operations || Yes (GIL released) | | Index | Rows are indexed || Integer positions are used | | Evaluation | Eager || Eager and lazy | | Query optimizer | No || Yes | | Out-of-core | No || Yes | | [SIMD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_instruction,_multiple_data) vectorization | Yes || Yes | | Data in memory | [NumPy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) arrays || [Apache Arrow](https://github.com/apache/arrow) arrays | | Memory efficiency | Poor || Excellent | | Missing data handling | Inconsistent || Consistent; type stability | ## Missing data {.center} ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="52%"} **pandas** For historical reasons, depending on dtype, missing values are one of: - `NaN` (`numpy.nan`, dtype float) - `None` - `NaT` (Not-a-Time, dtype datetime) - `pandas.NA` (nullable scalar) This leads to inconsistent behaviour and unexpected dtype changes [Ongoing open issue since 2020](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/32265) ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="46%"} **Polars** One single missing value: `null` Simple and consistent behaviour across all data types and better performance `NaN` exists and is a float. It is **not** missing data, but the result of mathematical operations that don't return numbers [Here](https://www.dontusethiscode.com/blog/2025-01-22_null_vs_nan_applied.html) is a blog that goes into this in details ::: :::: ## Group by {.center} [Here](https://labs.quansight.org/blog/dataframe-group-by) is a great blog comparing the ease of use of passing expressions to groups in Polars compared to pandas *TL;DR Polars has a much more straightforward and efficient syntax* ## A note on my comparisons {.center} The performance of tools can only be evaluated on optimized code: poorly written pandas code is a lot slower than efficient pandas code To ensure fairness towards pandas, I use the best pandas code from [Alex Razoumov pandas course](https://wgpages.netlify.app/python2/python-13-pandas/) in which he benchmarks various syntaxes # Example 1: a classic ## FizzBuzz {.center} [FizzBuzz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fizz_buzz#Programming) is a programming exercise based on a children game which consists of counting to `n`, replacing: - any number divisible by `3` with `Fizz` - any number divisible by `5` with `Buzz` - any number divisible by both `3` and `5` with `FizzBuzz` Let's do it: ::: {#bf38f629 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Load libraries import pandas as pd import numpy as np import polars as pl # Set series length n = 10_000 ``` ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#b04c1ab4 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd = pd.DataFrame() df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, n+1) df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz" ``` ::: ## {.center} ::: {#b028505b .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
Count FizzBuzz
0 1 1
1 2 2
2 3 Fizz
3 4 4
4 5 Buzz
... ... ...
9995 9996 Fizz
9996 9997 9997
9997 9998 9998
9998 9999 Fizz
9999 10000 Buzz

10000 rows × 2 columns

``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#aec3b429 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl = df_pl.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) ``` ::: ## {.center} ::: {#1468b6c8 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
shape: (10_000, 2)
CountFizzBuzz
i64str
1"1"
2"2"
3"Fizz"
4"4"
5"Buzz"
……
9996"Fizz"
9997"9997"
9998"9998"
9999"Fizz"
10000"Buzz"
``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#bc2eed59 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code code-line-numbers="1-10|12"} df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) df_pl_lazy_collected = df_pl_lazy.collect() ``` ::: ## {.center} ::: {#2aaca2db .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy_collected ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ```{=html}
shape: (10_000, 2)
CountFizzBuzz
i64str
1"1"
2"2"
3"Fizz"
4"4"
5"Buzz"
……
9996"Fizz"
9997"9997"
9998"9998"
9999"Fizz"
10000"Buzz"
``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#3c51b7e7 .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pd = pd.DataFrame() df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, n+1) df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz" df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz" ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 4.31 ms ± 316 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#a82049fe .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 863 μs ± 163 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#f3874d5c .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)}) df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz")) df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns( pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz")) .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz")) .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz") ) df_pl = df_pl_lazy.collect() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 535 μs ± 56 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Speedup {.center} Speedup with Polars DataFrame around **6** \ With Polars LazyFrame around **8** These speedups highly depend on the types and sizes of the problems How much faster Polars is does vary, what doesn't vary is that it is always much faster ## Memory usage {.center} ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="32%"} **pandas** ::: {#65c68e41 .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd.memory_usage() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ``` Index 132 Count 80000 FizzBuzz 80000 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: 132 + 80,000 * 2 = \ 160,132 bytes \ **≈ 160 KB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="32%"} **Polars** ::: {#60db5716 .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` 119409 ``` ::: ::: 119,409 bytes \ **≈ 119 KB** 160,132 / 119,409 ≈ 1.3 \ Footprint 1.3 times smaller ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="32%"} **Polars Lazy API** Same as Polars eager: \ if you collect the entire DataFrame as we did here, the lazy API does not reduce the memory footprint ::: :::: # Example 2: medium dataset ## Data characteristics {.center} Shape: 216,930 rows, 7 columns ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#ff29adde .cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd = pd.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pd_subset = df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"] df_pd_subset.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=13} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#5ca2a0b6 .cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl = pl.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl_subset = df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY") df_pl_subset.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=14} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: ## Code {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#c00c0abe .cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl_lazy_subset_collected = df_pl_lazy.filter( pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY" ).collect() df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=15} ``` (349, 7) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#f995b698 .cell execution_count=17} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pd = pd.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"].shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 1.17 s ± 52.7 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#2be15fd0 .cell execution_count=18} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl = pl.read_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 757 ms ± 11.5 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each) ``` ::: ::: ## Timing {.center} ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"} ::: {#95eb48be .cell execution_count=19} ``` {.python .cell-code} %%timeit df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv( "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv" ) df_pl_lazy.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").collect().shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 64.5 ms ± 3.12 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each) ``` ::: ::: ## Speedup {.center} Polars here brings a speedup around **1.5** The Lazy API ramps that up to a speedup of **17** ## Memory usage {.center} ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="30%"} ::: {#50632b14 .cell execution_count=20} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pd.memory_usage() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=19} ``` Index 132 Show Number 1735440 Air Date 1735440 Round 1735440 Category 1735440 Value 1735440 Question 1735440 Answer 1735440 dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: 132 + 1,735,440 * 7 = \ 12,148,212 bytes \ **≈ 12 MB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="33%"} ::: {#5e7ebaae .cell execution_count=21} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=20} ``` 31606250 ``` ::: ::: 31,606,250 bytes \ **≈ 31 MB** 31,606,250 / 12,148,212 ≈ 2.6 \ Footprint 2.6 times *bigger* ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="33%"} ::: {#1b5d8ffc .cell execution_count=22} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=21} ``` 48774 ``` ::: ::: 48,774 bytes \ **≈ 49 KB** 12,148,212 / 48,774 ≈ 249 \ Footprint **249 times smaller** If you only collect a subset of the data, the memory footprint drops dramatically ::: :::: # Example 3:
data too big to fit in memory ## Example dataset {.center} Let's play with data from the [GBIF](https://www.gbif.org/) website for free and open access to biodiversity data The [Southern African Bird Atlas Project 2](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/906e6978-e292-4a8b-9c39-adf6bb0f3323) contains a raw CSV file of 42 GB and an interpreted CSV file of 12.3 GB. Let's look at the latter: Shape: 25,687,526 rows, 50 columns From that dataset, I want a list of species from the genus *Passer* (Old World sparrows) ## RAM required {.center} ::::{.columns} :::{.column width="49%"} **pandas** 132 + 205500208 * 50 = \ 10,275,010,532 bytes \ **≈ 10 GB** ::: :::{.column width="2%"} ::: :::{.column width="49%"} **Polars** 12,291,024,224 bytes \ **≈ 12 GB** ::: :::: Let's image that you have only 16 GB of RAM on your machine Running a browser and a few small applications take about 6 GB out of that. This dataset is already too big to process on your laptop If you need to play with the raw data or a larger dataset such as the [eBird dataset](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/4fa7b334-ce0d-4e88-aaae-2e0c138d049e) of 1,775,781,186 rows and 50 columns (**CSV file of 3 TB for the raw data**), you will need A LOT of memory if you use pandas ## Options {.center} In pandas or Polars: - you can select only the columns you are interested - you can read and process the file in chunks and try to combine the results If you only need to run queries on the data however, the Polars Lazy API is the answer ## Create a LazyFrame {.center} You can create a LazyFrame (no impact on memory, LazyFrame returned instantly): ::: {#0a0784ac .cell execution_count=23} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv("sa_birds.csv", separator="\t") ``` ::: Run a query on it (no impact on memory, LazyFrame returned instantly): ::: {#845d117f .cell execution_count=24} ``` {.python .cell-code} passer_df_lazy = df_pl_lazy.filter( pl.col("genus") == "Passer" ).select(pl.col("species")).unique() ``` ::: ## Collect result {.center} Now, you collect the result (this uses memory and takes time to compute) and turn the DataFrame into a Python list: ::: {#0d245f49 .cell execution_count=25} ``` {.python .cell-code} passer_ls = passer_df_lazy.collect().get_column("species").to_list() ``` ::: Depending on the subset returned by your query, the memory usage will vary Here, it is minuscule (79 bytes): ::: {#69c3b10e .cell execution_count=26} ``` {.python .cell-code} passer_df_lazy.collect().estimated_size() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=25} ``` 79 ``` ::: ::: ## Et voilà {.center} ::: {#633684e7 .cell execution_count=27} ``` {.python .cell-code} print(passer_ls) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ['Passer motitensis', 'Passer melanurus', 'Passer diffusus', 'Passer domesticus', 'Passer griseus'] ``` ::: ::: With this method, as long as the data itself fits on a drive and the queries don't return huge subsets of data, you can run queries on giant datasets such as the 3 TB [eBird dataset](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/4fa7b334-ce0d-4e88-aaae-2e0c138d049e) with a small amount of RAM Note that the queries can contain complex expressions. What matters is the size of the subset you need to collect at the end
